summary: Atwood examines how Web 2.0 platforms profit from user-generated content while users work for free, drawing parallels to sharecropping. He highlights how sites like MySpace and Facebook provide tools for self-expression while retaining ownership and economic rewards. The post questions whether this arrangement is exploitative, noting that individual contributions have trivial economic value but become lucrative when aggregated at massive scale. Users remain 'happy' because they seek social connection rather than profit.
key_points: Web 2.0 companies distribute the means of production but concentrate economic rewards among themselves
Users willingly contribute free labor because their motivation is self-expression and socializing, not money
Individual user contributions have trivial economic value; only massive aggregation creates lucrative businesses
The 'digital sharecropping' model parallels historical sharecropping where workers don't own what they produce
money_quotes: MySpace, Facebook, and many other businesses have realized that they can give away the tools of production but maintain ownership over the resulting products.
One of the fundamental economic characteristics of Web 2.0 is the distribution of production into the hands of the many and the concentration of the economic rewards into the hands of the few.
It's a sharecropping system, but the sharecroppers are generally happy because their interest lies in self-expression or socializing, not in making money.
It's only by aggregating those contributions on a massive scale – on a web scale – that the business becomes lucrative.
key_insight: Web 2.0 platforms have created a new form of digital sharecropping where users happily provide free labor for self-expression while companies capture all the economic value through massive aggregation.
